What is cross-entropy loss?

• Cross-entropy measures the divergence between the predicted probability distribution and the true distribution
• For classification, it equals -log(p_correct_class)
• Minimising it is equivalent to maximising log-likelihood of the correct label under the model

Explain dropout regularisation.

Dropout randomly zeros a fraction of activations during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations. At inference, all units are active but outputs are scaled. It approximates ensemble averaging over exponentially many sub-networks.

You are a technical writer. Always structure responses as: 1. Definition, 2. Example, 3. Limitation. Use exactly this structure, even for simple questions.
What is overfitting?

1. Definition: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, capturing noise rather than underlying patterns, causing poor performance on new data.

2. Example: A decision tree with unlimited depth memorises every training point, achieving 100% training accuracy but failing on any slightly different test input.

3. Limitation: Overfitting is hard to detect during training since training loss keeps decreasing. It only becomes visible when validation loss diverges from training loss.

What is cross-entropy loss?

Cross-entropy measures the divergence between the predicted probability distribution and the true distribution. For classification, it equals -log(p_correct_class). Minimising it is equivalent to maximising log-likelihood of the correct label under the model.

What is overfitting?

1. Definition: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, capturing noise rather than underlying patterns, causing poor performance on new data.

2. Example: A decision tree with unlimited depth memorises every training point, achieving 100% training accuracy but failing on any slightly different test input.

3. Limitation: Overfitting is hard to detect during training since training loss keeps decreasing. It only becomes visible when validation loss diverges from training loss.

What is overfitting?

1. Definition: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, capturing noise rather than underlying patterns, causing poor performance on new data.

2. Example: A decision tree with unlimited depth memorises every training point, achieving 100% training accuracy but failing on any slightly different test input.

3. Limitation: Overfitting is hard to detect during training since training loss keeps decreasing. It only becomes visible when validation loss diverges from training loss.

What is overfitting?

1. Definition: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, capturing noise rather than underlying patterns, causing poor performance on new data.

2. Example: A decision tree with unlimited depth memorises every training point, achieving 100% training accuracy but failing on any slightly different test input.

3. Limitation: Overfitting is hard to detect during training since training loss keeps decreasing. It only becomes visible when validation loss diverges from training loss.

You are a technical writer. Always structure responses as: 1. Definition, 2. Example, 3. Limitation. Use exactly this structure, even for simple questions.
What is overfitting?

1. Definition: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, capturing noise rather than underlying patterns, causing poor performance on new data.

2. Example: A decision tree with unlimited depth memorises every training point, achieving 100% training accuracy but failing on any slightly different test input.

3. Limitation: Overfitting is hard to detect during training since training loss keeps decreasing. It only becomes visible when validation loss diverges from training loss.
